coredata.dc:description: TiO2 nanowire arrays (TNWAs) thin film of flexible substrates has great application potential in photoelectrochemical (PEC) hydrogen evolution. Moreover, the photoquantum efficiency of TNWAs can be effectively enhanced by reducing the nanoscale as much as possible or combining with other co-catalysts. This work presents a simple and effective strategy for the synthesis of ultrafine TiO2 nanowire arrays by a one-step hydrothermal method using CQDs as an inducer for single crystal growth. Meanwhile, CQDs recombine on the surface of TNWAs to form CQDs/TNWAs heterojunction during the synthesis process. The synthesized TNWAs have well ordered separability, whose average diameter is regulated at 11.21 nm with an average aspect ratio of 1097. PEC analysis showed that the performance of TNWAs was significantly improved compared with initial TiO2 with unregulated morphology. The charge separation efficiency increased by 120%, applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) reached 0.37%(0.68 v vs RHE), and incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) reached 50.31% (380 nm). The influence of TiO2 nano-morphology optimization and formation of CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction on the PEC performance were discussed in detail.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots induced one-dimensional ordered growth of single crystal TiO2 nanowires while boosting photoelectrochemistry properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 nanowire arrays (TNWAs) thin film of flexible substrates has great application potential in photoelectrochemical (PEC) hydrogen evolution. Moreover, the photoquantum efficiency of TNWAs can be effectively enhanced by reducing the nanoscale as much as possible or combining with other co-catalysts. This work presents a simple and effective strategy for the synthesis of ultrafine TiO2 nanowire arrays by a one-step hydrothermal method using CQDs as an inducer for single crystal growth. Meanwhile, CQDs recombine on the surface of TNWAs to form CQDs/TNWAs heterojunction during the synthesis process. The synthesized TNWAs have well ordered separability, whose average diameter is regulated at 11.21 nm with an average aspect ratio of 1097. PEC analysis showed that the performance of TNWAs was significantly improved compared with initial TiO2 with unregulated morphology. The charge separation efficiency increased by 120%, applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) reached 0.37%(0.68 v vs RHE), and incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) reached 50.31% (380 nm). The influence of TiO2 nano-morphology optimization and formation of CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction on the PEC performance were discussed in detail.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemistry (PEC) device is applied in the field of inspection and detection, energy conversion, environmental governance, etc. In particular, the applied value of hydrogen production is being recognized. The central part of the PEC device is semiconductor materials. Although 50 years have passed since the first pioneering work by Fujishima and Honda [1], TiO2 as a benchmark of semiconductor photocatalytic has always maintained high clout due to its stable photochemical properties, high catalytic efficiency, non-toxic, low cost and no secondary pollution [2]. Therefore, the research potential of TiO2 is continuously explored.

Both electrocatalysts and photocatalysts can be applied to hydrogen evolution. The activity of electrocatalysis depends on the surface geometry and electronic structure [3,4], while the activity of photocatalysis depends on its photoelectric properties. separation of electron-hole pairs [5,6], recombination rate of photoelectron-hole [7], photoelectron lifetime [8,9], efficiency of electron transfer [10,11] and spectral response range [12,13] are important parameters to evaluate the performance of semiconductor materials. Accordingly, considerable efforts have been devoted to various nanocrystallization technologies of TiO2 to get excellent charge transport capabilities and photoelectric yield[14,15], including nano particles [16], nanotubes [17], nanosheets [18] and nanowires [14,19,20]. Widely used TiO2 nano-powders (eg. P25) cannot be separated and recycled in body of water due to their nanoscale, which severely limits their application. By contrast, TiO2 nano thin films offer a good solution. Among various nanostructures, highly ordered nanowire arrays can provide more optical pathways in the longitudinal direction than nanosheets, nanotubes and nanoparticles, which can make the separation, transmission and collection of photoelectron much easier than the other nanostructures [21–25]. Reports shows that the PEC performance of nanowires depends on aspect ratio [10,26,27] and nanoscale [28]. Jie Liu et al. [10,23,26] indicated that higher electron diffusion efficiency could be achieved by changing the morphology of nanowires to be thinner, higher aspect ratio, better separation, and more orderly. Similar conclusions have been drawn from related work by other researchers [21,22,29–31]. Therefore, TiO2 nanowire arrays (TNWAs) with ultrafine nanostructures (<15 nm), high aspect ratio (˃1000) and well separation are the most ideal structure. However, to date, the TNWAs which possess the above characteristics haven’t been reported. Recently, Tingsheng Zhou et al. [15,32] presented a kind of TiO2 nanorods on FTO with well-separated structures by a hydrothermal method, the film consists of dense TiO2 nanorods with average width of 150 nm and length of 3.0 µm, then CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction was constructed, but the performance of TiO2 matrix was not excellent enough. This is probably due to the large size of the nanorods, which limits the quantum yield to further improvement. Wenwu Liu et al. [21,22,30] reported well-separated and nanostructured nanowires, which grew in-situ on Ti film by hydrothermal, and the diameter can be controlled within 70 nm. So far, the minimum diameter of TiO2 nanowires (TNW) was reported to be 10–15 nm[33] and the maximum aspect ratio was 793 [26]. Therefore, in our opinion, the morphology of TNWAs still has great potential for further optimization in the diameter refinement (<15 nm), structural ordering, and aspect-ratio enhancement.

TiO2 also has obvious drawbacks, such as low visible light activity and high recombination rate of electron-hole, which is due to its wide bandgap (∼3.2 eV for anatase TiO2) and short hole diffusion length (10 −100 nm) [15,34–38]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a novel quasi-zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterial, have a uniqueness of photoluminescence due to the quantum size effect and dielectric confinement effect [39]. Meanwhile, CQDs are gradually shining in the field of photocatalysis [40,41]. Recent researches suggested that an appropriate amount of CQDs combined with TiO2 could boost the PEC performance of TiO2, which was mainly determined by several aspects: (i) CQDs provide a fast transfer channel for electrons and accelerate charge transfer dynamics, while reducing the probability of electron-hole pair recombination [42–48]. (ii) CQDs can also enhance the reaction site by amplifying the specific area [44]. (iii) CQDs plays an upconversion role on TiO2, thus improving the optical absorption range of TiO2 [49–52]，however, this view is still controversial. (iv) CQDs and TiO2 can construct a Z-type reaction heterojunction, to optimize electron transition paths and improve the separation of electron-hole pairs [15].

In order to get finer, longer and more ordered TNWAs, TNW should be encouraged to one-dimensional normal growth, while two-dimensional growth need to be inhibited. Existed reports [53–56] had shown that weak interaction between zinc metal and zinc ions lead to lateral dendrite growth, and the Zn deposition wasn’t uniform. The solution was to add active substances rich in functional groups (-COO, -OH, -O), which were complexed with Zn2+ to induce the directional crystal growth. Inspired by this, according to our group's previous study [57–59], CQDs may be an ideal candidate for this active substance. CQDs are a kind of semiconductor-like luminescent material whose surface is easy to be passivated [60]. The CQDs surface are rich in hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups (-OH, -COOH, etc.) that can be well dispersed in water. In addition, CQDs can bond not only with most metallic ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn4+, Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, etc.) but also with functional groups (-OH, -CN, -NH2, -COOH, -NO2, -CO, etc.), which is beneficial to induce crystal nucleation and growth. From this, it’s possibility that CQDs enable TNWAs one-dimensional growth, however, so far, there is no research report on this aspect.

Herein, in this work, we first use CQDs as growth regulators, titanium (Ti) foil as a Ti source to synthesize TWNAs by one-step hydrothermal. The synthesized TNWAs make a simultaneous morphological breakthrough in the following aspects: (i) ultra-fine mean diameter size of less than 12 nm (the smallest diameter of 12.3 nm ever reported [14]), (ii) well separation without the compact layer of TiO2 near the substrate. (iii) high aspect ratio of 1097 (the maximum value of 793 ever reported [26]). In this process, CQDs paly two roles: one is as a growth regulator to control the growth of TNW along the normal direction, and the other is to improve the photocatalytic performance as a cocatalyst. In existing reports, there are few studies on the contribution rate of TiO2 PEC performance by morphology optimization and heterojunction optimization. In this paper, base on discussion of the PEC performance of photoelectrodes, we will put forward our views from six aspects: separation and recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs, photoelectronic lifetime, charge-transfer efficiency, spectral response range and hydrogen-evolution performance respectively, and the mechanisms of involved experimental phenomena are discussed in detail.

Ti foil (purity: 99.996%, thickness:0.1 mm) was purchased from Baoti Group Co. Ltd (Baoji China), which processing state is cold rolled without stress release or annealing. Glucose was produced by Shanghai McLean Co. Ltd. Other reagents include Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), nitric acid (HNO3), hydrochloric acid (HCl), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), ammonium fluoride (NH4F), anhydrous ethanol (C₂H5OH) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co. LTD (Guangzhou China). All reagents are of analytical or higher grade and were used directly without further purification. Deionized water was used during the synthesizing process, and ultrapure water was used during PL (photoluminescence) and UV–visible absorption tests.

Synthesis of CQDs was accomplished by the hydrothermal method. The glucose aqueous solution (0.1 mol/L) was transferred to a stainless steel reactor with a Para Phenylene Phenol (PPL) liner. Then it was placed in an oven at 180 ℃ for 12 h and cooled to room temperature. The obtained yellowish-brown liquid is CQDs solution.
The CQDs solution stored in the refrigerator at 5 ℃ for more than 72 h to keep surface passivation and functional groups in a stable state, which can be used directly for the following synthesis.

CQDs/TNWAs were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The titanium foil was cut into small pieces of 2.5 cm × 4 cm with a plate cutter, and then immersed in anhydrous ethanol at 70 ℃ with ultrasonic cleaning for 10 min to remove the residual surface rolling oil. The cleaned Ti foil was immersed in the pretreatment solution composed of HNO3, H2O2, NH4F and H2O to get a rough-textured surface. After cleaning again with deionized water, the Ti foils were transferred to a stainless steel hydrothermal kettle with a PPL liner. The reaction liquid is composed of different concentration of NaOH (1 ∼ 5 M) mixed with different contents of CQDs (0 mg/L, 240 mg/L, 480 mg/L, 720 mg/L, and 960 mg/L), then heated to 200 ℃ in the oven for 20 h, and cooled to room temperature. The synthetic intermediate product with nanostructure was washed with deionized water and immersed in 1 M HCl for 15 min to replace Na+ with H+ to form Hydrogen titanate (H2TixO2x+1·H2O) [21,22,28]. Here, to prevent oxidation of CQDs during heat treatment, we used high-purity aluminium foil as sacrificial agent, wrapped the quartz crucible for holding samples in the aluminium foil layer by layer, and heat-treated at 500◦C for 1 h in a tubular furnace with argon atmosphere. At this point, CQDs/H2TixO2x+1·H2O was converted to CQDs/TNWAs by removing crystal water and constitution water. Schematic diagram of CQDs/TNWAs preparation is shown in Scheme 1.

In order to verify the synergistic effect of CQDs on the photocatalytic performance of TNWAs. Air calcination was used instead of the inert atmosphere annealing to synthesize TNWAs without CQDs. Here, the intermediate (CQDs/H2TixO2x+1·H2O) was burned in muffle furnace for 1 h in air atmosphere of 500◦C. At this point, the CQDs-removed samples were taken out and named as TNWAs.

The morphology of samples was analyzed by field emissioned scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Zeiss, Gemini 300, Carl Zeiss & Company/Oxford, Germany) at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. TiO2 lattice image and CQDs distribution position were obtained by field emissioned high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, 2100 F, JEOL, Japan), operated at 200 kV. The micro-distribution of elements were analyzed by Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The phase was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD: PANalytical PRO PANalytical Empyrean, Netherlands). Chemical bonding and chemical shift were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA.), using monochromatic Alkα (1486.6 eV) as the ray source, the passing energy spectrum of the peak splitting is 20 eV, the step size is 0.1 eV, all peaks were corrected with respect to C-C/CC (284.8 eV). The functional group distribution was analyzed by Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700-NXR, USA). The ultraviolet absorption spectrum was measured by ultraviolet-visible light-near infrared spectrophotometer (UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer, Perkin Elmer Lambda 750, Shanghai, China), the scanning range was 200 ∼ 600 nm, among them, TNWAs films were analyzed using diffuse reflection integrating spheres. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CQDs solution were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent VARIAN, USA.). The PL and transient time-resolved fluorescence (TRF) spectra of solid films were recorded by steady-state and lifetime spectrophotometers (quantmaster8000, HORIBA, USA.). Other details of test parameters would be given in discussion.

In this work, the photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance test was completed by using AM 1.5 G 300 W xenon lamp (PerfectLight PLS-SXE300 +/UV, CHINA) and 250 W xenon lamp with optical fiber (CEAULIGHT TCX-250, CHINA.). The test was carried out in a three-electrode with a high purity quartz (˃99.9%) electrolytic cell. The PEC parameters were evaluated by an electrochemical workstation (Admiral SquidStat Plus, USA.) The three-electrode consists of a counter electrode (platinum foil), a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl electrode filled with saturated KCl), and a working electrode (L-shaped working electrode with working surface of 1 cm2), respectively. The reference potential of Ag/AgCl electrode was converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) potential according to the Nernst Eqs. (1,2) [30].
(1) ERHE = ENHE + 0·059 × pH
(2) ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0·1976
Where, ENHE is the converted potential vs. RHE, 0.059 is constant, pH indicates the pH of the electrolyte at 25 °C, 0.1976 represents the difference value between EAg/AgCl and ESHE (standard hydrogen electrode). The electrolyte was 0.5 mol of Na2SO4 (pH=5.6), and test temperature was 25◦C. The photocurrent-potential curve (J-V) was measured by scanning linear voltammetry (LSV) at the scanning rate of 10 mV/s, and Na2SO3 was used as hole scavenger for texting ηbulk and ηsurface. Applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE %) was calculated by Eq. (3) [61].
(3) ABPE (%) = I (1·23− Vbias) / Jlight ×100%
Where, I is response photocurrent at bias voltage, Vbias is the bias potential vs. RHE, Jlight is irradiance density (100 mW/cm2). The flat band potential was obtained by Motschottky (M-S) test with a potential increment of 0.05 V and a frequency of 1000 Hz in the dark. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy tests were taken out under light (40 mW/cm2) with a potential of 0.16 V and a frequency range of 10−2 ∼ 105 Hz.

The incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was measured using xenon lamp with grating monochromator (WDS-8 Tuopu, China) and optical power meter ( PerfectLight PL-MW2000, China). The wavelength range was set from 300 to 600 nm at 1.23 V vs. RHE. The IPCE was calculated according to Eq. (4) [15].
(4) ICPE (%) = 1240 Jph / λ Plight ×100%
Where, Jph, λ and Plight are photocurrent density, incident light wavelength and incident light power density respectively. The photocurrent and stability were tested using the I-T method and applying a bias voltage of + 0.3 V (Chenhua CHI660D, China), the illumination intensity was about 40 m W/cm, and the shutter interval was 60 s

Hydrogen evolution experiment were carried out in a quartz reactor connecting to a closed gas circulation system. The photoelectrodes were cut into 3 cm × 3 cm and placed in the reactor with 50 ml deionized water. Then the water was degassed for 30 min by argon, and continuously stir deionized water using magnetic agitator, followed by irradiation with the 300 W Xe lamp for 5 h. The generated hydrogen was connected to the gas chromatograph (Agilent 8890, American) through the gas on-line sampling valve for measurement.

As shown in the TEM image ( Fig. 1a), the prepared CQDs are uniformly-dispersed spherical nanoparticles without agglomeration, combined with the inset in Fig. 1c, the CQDs solution is yellowish-brown without precipitation or stratification, indicating the synthesized CQDs are evenly distributed and well dispersed. The enlarged inset shows its lattice fringes, the lattice spacing is measured as 0.211 nm, corresponding to the C(100) plane [32,42]. The diameter is mostly distributed between 3 and 6 nm with an average particle size of 5.13 nm (Fig. 1b). The PL spectra of CQDs are shown in Fig. 1c, with the excitation wavelength moving from 325 to 450 nm, the continuous red-shift of the emission peak indicates the dependence of excitation wavelength. Maximum intensity of fluorescence emission occurs at excitation wavelength near 350 nm, here, photoelectron first undergoes ultrafast nonradiative transition to a lower band and decays to the ground state through radiative exciton recombination, which yields PL centred at 440 nm [52]. Similarly, when excitation wavelength changes to 450 nm, the radiative transition is almost negligible, such luminous phenomenon is due to the electron energy levels from π to π * in the sp2 are affected by the σ and σ * states of the sp3 substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, composition and light penetration of CQDs/TNWAs
ce:section.ce:para: In the one-step hydrothermal method, different contents (240 mg/L, 480 mg/L, 720 mg/L, 960 mg/L) of CQDs were added into the hydrothermal reaction solution to verify the influence of CQDs concentration on the growth morphology of TNW. Fig. 2 shows the SEM morphology of synthesized CQDs/TiO2/Ti. As illustrated in Fig. 2a,b， when no CQDs regulator are added to the reaction solution, the synthesized TiO2 exhibited tiny nanosheets morphology. From the cross-sectional image, the overall thickness of the film is about 4.34 µm, which is composed of a surface loose layer and a subsurface compact layer (Fig. S1). For this morphology, since only the loose layer can be penetrated by light, the photocatalytic performance largely depends on this part. The formation of the compact layer is due to the two-dimension growth of TiO2. With the participation of 240 mg/L CQDs in the reaction, the nanosheets gradually evolved into nanowires, eventually formed a hybrid structure of nanosheets and nanowires (Fig. 2c, d). And the thickness of CQDs/TNS & CQDs/TNW increased to 5.31 µm. Meanwhile, the compact layer began to loosen. When the presence of CQDs in the reaction increased to 480 mg/L, nanosheets are further transformed into nanowires. CQDs/TNW are gradually connected into a net structure leaving only a small amount of CQDs/TWS (Fig. 2e, f), and the overall thickness increased to 6.78 µm, at this point, the compact layer became looser. When the CQDs are further increased to 720 mg/L, the sample surface was completely covered by evenly interlacing CQDs/TNW (Fig. 2g, h), the total film thickness increased to 12.3 µm that was entirely composed of well-separated nanowire arrays (CQDs/TNWAs). Meanwhile, the compact layer disappeared, where light can be penetrated in the longitudinal depth. Therefore, the active specific area was greatly increased. Combined with the local magnified image (Fig. 2i) and the low magnification TEM image (Fig. 2j), the average diameter was calculated to be 11.21 nm. Based on the total thickness (12.30 µm), the average aspect ratio is estimated to be as high as 1097. Additionally, Fig. 2k shows the TNW formed by excessive addition of CQDs during the reaction, the nanowires aggregated together, resulting in the disappearance of the array morphology. At this time, the total thickness of the film was reduced to 7.15 µm, which was attributed to some bending of the nanowires as TNW become sparse. There is a view[33] that CQDs will hinder the growth of TiO2, but that is not entirely true. Based on the above phenomenon, it can be concluded that two-dimensional growth including normal growth and lateral growth is the basic growth law of TNW. And the stacking of nanosheets will form compact layers, which severely limits the light penetration and specific surface area improvement. Hereon, the one-dimensional growth of TiO2 can be guided effectively by adding an appropriate amount of CQDs to the reaction, making the nanowires thinner, longer, and more orderly. However, excessive CQD will cover the nucleation sites of partial TiO2 and hinder nanowire growth.

Here, we adopted a simple reflective experiment to verify the effect of compact layer on light penetration. Fig. S2 shows the reflection of light. The results indicate that although the thickness of TNWAs film is nearly three times as large as TNS, it has excellent light penetration, which is benefited from the highly ordered array of ultrafine nanowires. Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of CQDs/TiO2 synthesized in different CQDs concentrations (0 ∼ 960 mg/L) by the hydrothermal method. From the analysis of diffraction spectrum, the main phase of the synthesized TiO2/Ti is anatase (101) and (200), which is consistent with PDF #21–1272, accompanying by a small amount of rutile (111). From CQDs diffraction spectrum, a broad peak appearing around 23.5° indicate the amorphous carbon with noncrystalline structure. Moreover, with the increase of CQDs involved in hydrothermal reaction, the growth of anatase (200) and rutile (111) crystal face are inhibited. Accordingly, anatase (101) face shows a strong preference orientation. By software (Jade 5.0) measurement, from the original sample (TNS, 0 mg/L) to CQDs/TNWAs (720 mg/L), the content percentage of the anatase (101) phase increased from 56.51% to 92.32%, that of anatase (200) phase decreased from 37.59% to 3.4%, and that of rutile (111) phase decreased from 3.94% to 1.08%. It is remarkable the peak of anatase (101) appears to widen, which does not mean that the nanowires grow in a polycrystalline state, most likely, the nanosheets are gradually transformed into smaller nanowires. Moreover, the positions of all characteristic peaks did not shift, indicating that CQDs didn’t enter into the crystal lattice of TiO2.

To get the distribution of CQDs in the synthetic CQDs/TNWAs, TEM analysis was performed on the optimal sample 720CQDs/TNWAs. Fig. 4a, b show two distribution of CQDs, and the inset shows the SAED pattern of TNWAs, indicating the synthesized TNWAs belong to single crystal structure. Fig. 4a shows two single-crystal nanowires, which correspond to anatase (101), and CQDs are distributed diffusely on their surface. Fig. 4b shows CQDs are attached to the surface of nanowires. In Fig. 4c, a small amount of rutile phase (200) are found, which combined with anatase (101) to form a heterojunction. The presence of rutile (200) is consistent with the XRD results. The elemental mapping of CQDs/TNWAs is shown in Fig. 4d ∼ g. The distribution of the three elements of Ti, O, C is given in the figure, which confirms that the carbon element is uniformly distributed. However, carbon element is detected in other positions except for TNWAs, which is caused by using C supporting film during the test. In order to estimate the content of element C while avoiding the influence of substrate impurities, the SEM-EDS test was further supplemented (Fig. S3). By subtracting the pollution effects of carbon, the Ti:O:C atom ratios of 720CQDs/TNWAs and TNWAs are 32.26: 63.91: 0.83 and 32.16: 61.75: 0.09, respectively. The results indicate TiO2 is the synthetic product. Corresponding mass ratios by conversion are 156: 104: 1 and 1429: 915: 1, respectively. The results suggest that the loading capacity of CQDs on 720 CQDs/TNWAs was 0.38%, and the residual amount on TNWAs was 0.04%, indicating that CQDS was almost removed by air calcined. Furthermore, the element mapping of TNWAs also supports this (Fig. S4). In summary, we confirm that the main distribution state of C is diffuse and discontinuous, and such discontinuous distribution of quantum dots is the key to improve the photoelectric performance.

To clarify the mechanism of CQDs regulating the growth of TNWAs, the reaction solution and intermediate products (Na2TixO2x+1) after hydrothermal reaction were studied respectively. The hydrothermal reaction solution was studied by PL spectra to verify whether CQDs could respond to Ti-containing groups. For comparison, two solution samples after hydrothermal reaction with the same preparation method as the 720CQDs/TNWAs were tested. Among them, 1# was a blank sample contained only CQDs and NaOH without Ti foil, and 2# contained CQDs, NaOH and Titanate ion (TiO4 4-). As shown in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of solution 2# is significantly reduced relative to solution 1#, and the fluorescence quenching rate is estimated to reach 61.9%. According to the previous research of our group[57–59], the Na+ ion won’t affect the fluorescence quenching of CQDs. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching is attributed to the bonding between CQDs and TiO4 4- FTIR was further tested to characterize functional groups and bonding of CQDs, Na2TixO2x+1, and CQDs/Na2TixO2x+1. As shown in Fig. 5b, there are abundant groups on the surface of CQDs. The broad absorption peaks at 3400 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of O-H, the peaks at 1430 cm−1 demonstrate the existence of skeletal vibrations of CC[53], and absorption the peaks at 1670 cm−1 and 1080 cm−1 are the stretchable vibration region of carbonyl (CO) and (C-O) respectively[32]. Both Na2TixO2x+1 and CQDs/Na2TixO2x+1 have a broad absorption peak in the range of 500 ∼ 800 cm−1, which is attributed to the bending vibration band of (Ti-O-Ti)[32,44]. With the addition of CQDs, there is a small absorption fluctuation at 1040 cm−1, which is judged to be tensile vibration of the C-O-Ti bond[32]. The formation of C-O-Ti means that a bridge with interaction between CQDs and Ti has been established.

In order to retain the chemical bond and chemisorption bond on the sample surface as much as possible, the intermediate products (Na2TixO2x+1) were immersed in deionized water for cleaning and natural dried. For comparison, two intermediates (Na2TixO2x+1 and CQDs/Na2TixO2x+1) were tested by XPS separately. Excluding the ghost peak, the characteristic peaks of Ti, O, C and Na can be observed in the full spectrum of XPS, as shown in Fig. 5c. According to the area of the characteristic peaks combined with the relative sensitivity factor (RSF) method, the relative atomic percentage of the four elements can be calculated. The results are shown in Table S1, the atomic ratio of Na, Ti and O of Na2TixO2x+1 is 22.10: 19.29: 48.61, and that of CQDs/Na2TixO2x+1 is Na:Ti:O= 17.13: 21.12: 56.75. So the intermediate products can be qualitatively for Na2Ti2O5 and CQDs/Na2Ti2O5. Here, it can be found that the addition of CQDs results in the insufficient content of Na+ ion. Given the results of PL and FTIR, it can be concluded that CQDs not only bond with Na2Ti2O5 but also adsorb TiO4 4- in reaction solution, which leads to the increase of Ti and O elements, and the decrease of Na ratio accordingly. Fig. 5d ∼ f shows the narrow spectrum fitting of C1s, O1s, and Ti 2 P respectively. From Fig. 5d, both Na2Ti2O5 and CQDs/Na2Ti2O5 show bimodal peaks of Ti 2 P 2/3 and Ti 2 P 1/2 caused by spin-orbit splitting, and the centres are located at 464.50 eV ∼ 458.81 eV and 464.68 eV ∼ 458.94 eV respectively, indicating the Ti elements are all + 4 valence. The reason for the generation of satellite peaks (Shakeup peak at 471.6 eV and 472 eV) are that external electrons of Na2Ti2O5 just prepared by hydrothermal have not been in a stable state. It can be found that Na2Ti2O5 has three fitting peaks corresponding to bound oxygen (Ti-O), surface adsorbed hydroxyl oxygen (-OH)[3,4,32] and a small amount of crystal water (H2O)[46] from Fig. 5e. When CQDs are present in the reaction, the positions of the O1 s peaks doesn’t change, but the adsorbed hydroxyl (-OH) of CQDs/Na2Ti2O5 increase nearly twice than that of Na2Ti2O5. The adsorbed hydroxyl (-OH) group consists of two parts, one is derived from the adsorption of hydroxyl groups on the surface of Na2Ti2O5, and the other is attributed to the bonding of the surface CQDs to the hydroxyl group. As shown in Fig. 5f, three peaks of Na2Ti2O5 centred at 284.8 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.2 eV are classified as the CC, C-OH and CO groups, corresponding proportions are 90%, 5.41% and 4.59%, respectively. In addition, the carboxyl group (O-CO) appeared at 289.07 eV. Compared with Na2Ti2O5, the hydroxyl (C-OH) content of CQDs/Na2Ti2O5 has increased by 2 times. And most of the carbonyl (CO) was replaced by carboxyl group (O-CO), verifying the strong interaction between CQDs and oxygen-containing functional group.

Based on above discussion and existing reports, we propose a possible growth mechanism of TNWAs. The preparation of crystals by hydrothermal method follows the basic principle of dissolution-precipitation. Therefore, the generation processes of the hydrothermal product Na2Ti2O5 in this work are shown in Eq. (5) and Eq. (6).
(5) Dissolution: Ti + 4Na++ 4OH- —— TiO4 4- + 4Na+
(6) Precipitation: 5TiO4 4- + 3Ti4++ 8Na+ —— 4Na2Ti2O5 (precipitates)

For nanowire crystal, the driving force of the normal growth is much greater than that of the lateral growth. However, during the growth of nanowires, many oxygen-containing such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl[53–55] can be absorbed on the nanowires’ surface, which would become ligand for absorbing metal ions[10], resulting in the nucleation and growth occur laterally. The phenomenon is attributed to the oxygen-contain function groups as electron-rich Lewis base can strongly interact with the electron-deficit Lewis acidic Ti4+ [53]. The above discussion shows that CQDs have a large number of adsorption bonds with these oxygen-containing functional groups, especially hydroxyl (-OH). These adsorption bonds connect CQDs with Na2Ti2O5 nanowires matrix, resulting in a great deal of CQDs attached to the surface of the matrix. When there are enough CQDs in the reaction solution, these surface-attached CQDs will displace Ti4+ ions and block the connection between Ti4+ and oxygen-containing functional groups, thereby inhibiting lateral growth. On the other hand, due to normal direction growth having a strong driving force, and immune to weak adsorption from CQDs, which enables one-dimensional growth of the nanowires along the normal direction, Fig. 5g shows schematic illustration of CQDs-induced TNWAs growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of structural evolution on PEC properties
ce:section.ce:para: Above results showed that TNS could evolve into TNWAs by CQDs regulator. To clarify the influence of morphology evolution on PEC properties, the CQDs are removed from the surface of 240CQDs/TNS&TNW, 480CQDs/TNS&TNW, 720CQDs/TNWAs and 960CQDs/TNW by the mentioned method in Section 2.2.3. The prepared samples were analyzed for PEC performance and re-labeled as 240TNS&TNW, 480TNS&TNW, 720TNWAs and 960TNW respectively. Fig. 6a shows chopped photocurrent-time responses. It can be seen with the evolution from nanosheets to nanowires, the photocurrent presents an increasing trend. The photocurrent of TNWAs increase by nearly 90% compared with TNS, implying that TNWAs produce more photocarriers. This is benefits from the higher specific surface area of TNWAs, which can better promote charge-hole separation[62–64]. It is worth mentioning that the photocurrent of 960TNW drops significantly, even approaching the level of TNS. In terms of surface topography, the excessive addition of CQDs leads to the collapse of arrays morphology, the thinning of nanowires, and the load of photocatalyst on the film decreases seriously, thus reducing the number of photocarriers.

To characterize the interfacial charge transfer kinetics, the EIS measurements were performed under irradiation. As shown in Fig. 6b, only one semicircle is detected, which is a typical Nyquist curve for metal oxides. The 720TNWAs have the smallest radius, indicating that it has lower charge transfer resistance and faster charge transfer kinetics [32,64]. Comparing with the photocurrent data, the photocurrent of 480TNS&TNW is higher than that of 960TNW, but the charge transfer resistance is indeed higher than 960TNW. According to the SEM analysis above, 480TNS&TNW has a compact layer, while 960TNW doesn’t, confirming that the well separation of nanowires without compact layer is more conducive to charge transfer, which is consistent with the conclusion of Jie Liu et al.[10].

In order to further characterize the synergistic relationship between Photoelectric yield and charge transfer efficiency, the photocurrent variation at different bias voltages (0 ∼ 2.5 V vs. RHE) was measured by LSV method (J-V curve) with light source of AM 1.5 G optical filter (Fig. S5b), the test results are shown in the Fig. 6c. On the whole, the photocurrent becomes nonlinearly rising with the increase of voltage. 720TNWAs show the largest positive slope, indicating the highest exchange current density between solid-liquid surface[4]. In addition, the current density of TNS displays a photocurrent density of ≈ 0.36 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. With the elimination of the nanosheets and the formation of discrete nanowires array, the photocurrent density of 720TNWAs is 90% higher than that of TNS, reaching 0.71 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. The low photocurrent of TNS could be attributed to the high charge transfer resistance of nanosheets under bias potential. Assuming 100% of Faradaic efficiency. Eq. (3) was used to calculate the ABPE of each sample. As shown in Fig. 6d, the optimal ABPE of TNS was only 0.14% at 0.686 V vs. RHE. With the evolution from TNS to TNW, the optimal APBE of 240TNS&TNW and 480TNS&TNW increased to 0.21% and 0.25% at 0.691 V vs. RHE and 0.69 V vs. RHE, respectively. 720TNWAs showed the maximum ABPE (0.31% at 0.686 V vs. RHE), which was higher than 0.13% [15] and 0.042% [32] reported of the TNWAs recently. From this, it can be concluded that the enhanced photoelectric current benefit from the optimized nano-morphology of TNWAs can better promote the hole-electron separation, thereby increasing the number of photogenerated carriers. On the other hand, the elimination of the dense layer close to the substrate and the optimization of the surface morphology synergistically accelerate the charge transfer kinetics, which is beneficial to the high-speed transfer of carriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CQDs synergistic catalysis on PEC properties
ce:section.ce:para: To further characterize the contribution of CQDs to the performance, three samples (TNS, 720TNWAs and 720CQDs/TNWAs) were selected as research objects, among them, the sample of 720TNWAs was annealed by air instead of protective atmosphere. For the purposes of discussion, they were simplistically labelled as TNS, TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs respectively. Here, PEC and hydrogen evolution properties of the three photoelectrodes were tested.

As shown in Fig. 7a, the photocurrent intensity of TNS, TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs are 0.575 mA/cm2, 0.495 mA/cm2 and 0.261 mA/cm2, compared with TNS, the photocurrent density of TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs increased by 90% and 120%, respectively. Based on existing literature[65,66], the number of photogenerated carriers can be used to evaluate the hole-electron separation efficiency. It can be conclude that the contributions of morphology optimization and CQDs compounding to the electron-hole separation efficiency of CQDs/TNWAs is 76% and 24% respectively. On the contrary, the Nyquist curve (Fig. 7b) shows that the CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction leads to a great reduction in charge transfer resistance, suggesting the contribution of CQDs are much higher than structural optimization. The acceleration of charge transfer kinetics is attributed to the excellent photoinduced charge transfer capability of CQDs[50,67,68]. Fig. 7c shows the transient chopper J-V response curve with a shutter switching period of 5 s, which intuitively shows the changing relationship between instantaneous response photocurrent and bias voltage. Accordingly, Fig. 7d shows J-V curve under continuous irradiation, the photocurrent density of CQDs/TNWAs is 0.88 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which are 2.44 and 1.24 times that of TNS (0.36 mA/cm2) and TNWAs (0.71 mA/cm2). The results are in good agreement with trends shown by I-t data (Fig. 7a), the contribution of morphological optimization is still greater than that of CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction.

The photoelectric conversion capacities of the photoanodes were evaluated by the ABPE, Eq. (3) is also used to calculate the ABPE value. It can be seen from Fig. 7e, the ABPE of CQDs/TNWAs was further increased to 0.37% at 0.681 V vs. RHE, which·19% and 164% higher than that of TNWAs and TNS, respectively. IPCE of photoelectrodes are calculated by Eq. (4) (Fig. 7f), which represents photon to current conversion efficiency in the incident light range of 320–600 nm. TNS, TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs all display the maximum IPCE at the wavelength of 380 nm, which are 28.74%, 43.5% and 50.31%, respectively. Compared with TNS, TNWAs showed stronger conversion efficiency at 360–400 nm due to its advantages in morphology. But it was almost the same in other wavelength, which was attributed to the inherent properties of TiO2. However, CQDs/TNWAs shows a difference, the IPCE was increased in the range of 320–410 nm compared to TNWAs, which confirmed the coupling strengthening of CQDs and TiO2. I-t method (Fig. 7g) was used to test the light stability of CQDs/TNWAs, the photocurrent density remained between 0.53 mA/cm2 and 0.55 mA/cm2 during the 8 h irradiation, and no photochemical corrosion occurred.

To further reveal the reason of the improved photoelectric efficiency by the structural optimization of TiO2 and the composition of CQDs with TiO2, bulk charge separation efficiency (η
bulk ) and surface charge transfer efficiency (η
surface ) of the TNS, TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs electrodes were measured. 1 mol/L Na2SO3 was added to the electrolyte as a hole scavenger to eliminate the charge injection barrier on the surface of the photoelectrode without affecting the charge separation in space charge layer, and measure the J-V curve (Fig. S5a). The η
bulk and η
surface are calculated by Eqs.(7) and (8) [69–71].

bulk of TNS, TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs are 36.44%, 46.2% and 54.58% at 1.23 V, and the corresponding η
surface of photoanodes is 33.35%, 42.85% and 63.74% at 1.23 V, respectively. Base on the results, both nanowire morphology and CQDs loading can enhance the separation of bulk charge and surface charge injection of TiO2. Thereinto, the η
surface of TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs is 9.5% and 30.39% higher than that of TNS, the results indicate CQDs contribute to the surface charge diffusion behavior of the photoelectrode, which is consistent with the Nyquist curve results.

To characterize the effect of CQDs on the concentration of injected holes of TiO2, The PEC hydrogen evolution properties of photoelectrodes were further tested. the results are shown in Fig. S6. In 3 h, the evolution amount of TNWAs (7.3 μmol) and CQDs/TNWAs (11.6 μmol) are 1.75 and 2.78 times of the TNS (4.17 μmol). This illustrates that the hole concentration participating in water oxidation of CQDs/TNWAs is significantly higher than the other two, which indirectly support to its significantly improved η
surface
. Repeated hydrogen evolution experiments of CQDs/TNWAs show a slight decline in efficiency, which decays to 11.17μmol and 10.73μmol, respectively. The efficiency attenuation of CQDs/TNWAs in 9 h is about 7.5%.

The results indicate that the composition of CQDs and the structural optimization of the film have different contributions to hole-carrier separation, charge transfer and degradation performance. In terms of hole-electron separation, TNWAs benefits from the morphological advantage of ultra-fine and ultra-high aspect ratio, which can generate a larger specific surface area, and the holes-electrons separation efficiency become higher. The contribution ratio (76%) of photocurrent generation is nearly 3 times higher than that of the CQDs combination (24%). It is also more advanced than reported PEC performance of various TiO2 Nanostructured film as show in Table S2. However, it doesn't mean such contribution can still maintain in practical application. Due to the lack of charge transfer medium, the more photoelectrons TNWAs produce, the more it recombines with the holes, which leads to the insignificant improvement of degradation performance. This is consistent with some views of existing literature[10,72]. Due to its inherent hydrophilicity, group adsorption characteristics, and photoelectric characteristics, CQDs are greatly improved in charge transferability and degradation performance after recombination with TNWAs.

Narrow spectra of C1s, O1s and Ti 2 P were tested by XPS as shown in Fig. 8 The C1s peak for TNWAs may originate from the absorbed hydrocarbon or contamination from XPS instruments[73]. Fig. 8a shows peak fitting results of C1s which are mainly divided into 284.8 eV, 285.93 eV and 288.39 eV, corresponding to the chemical bond of CC, C-O-C and CO. And that of CQDs/TNWAs divided into 284.8 eV, 286.11 eV and 288.94 eV, corresponding to CC, C-O-C and O-CO, which referred to Thermo Fisher XPS manual. The binding energy of CO shifted to positive by about 0.55 eV, attributing to the adsorption of hydroxyl being enhanced after TNWAs are loaded with CQDs. As shown in Fig. 8b, the O1s spectra of the TNWAs are fitted to three sub-peaks centred at 529.98 eV, 531.18 eV and 531.93 eV, corresponding to the oxygen element in TiO2 lattice (Olattice), oxygen vacancy (Ov) and the surface absorbed hydroxyl (O-OH), respectively. When TNWAs combine with CQDs, The content of Olattice and O-OH are both changed except Ov. The O-OH content of CQDs/ TNWAs (12.8%) is nearly 70% higher than that of TNWAs (7.6%), attributing to the coupling between CQDs and TiO2, which makes the surface easier to absorb oxygen-containing groups (-OH). It has been reported that the hydroxyl species at the surface of catalyst can enhance the OER activity[74]. In addition, the lattice shifted by 0.24 eV to the high binding energy state, indicating that CQDs interact with TiO2 to induce electron transfer from TiO2 to CQDs[32]. Fig. 8c shows the Ti 2 P fitting results of TNWAs and CQDs/ TNWAs, two peaks (Ti 2 P 2/3 and Ti 2 P 1/2 ) showed up caused by spin-orbit splitting, which centres at the binding energies of 458.69–464.38 eV and 458.88–464.48 eV, coinciding with Ti4+ valence in TiO2 lattice, besides, the element Ti didn’t form obvious chemical bonds with CQDs. The combination of CQDs resulted in a slight chemical shift of + 0.19 eV in the binding energy of Ti 2 P 2/3 , which is possibly attributed to the presence of Ti–C interaction[15]. To sum up, CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction can improve the surface hydroxyl adsorption capacity and induce charge transfer from TNWAs to CQDs. This represents a static case CQDs has unique photoluminescence properties and a wider absorption range. Therefore, the charge transfer characteristics may be different under irradiation.

To further characterize the influence of CQDs on the separation and transmission of photo-induced carriers, the steady-state and transient fluorescence spectra of TNS, TNWAs and CQDs/TNWWAs were measured. The PL spectra of the three samples are shown in Fig. 9a, the TNWAs show the highest fluorescence intensity, followed by the TNS and the CQDs/TNWAs. The results indicate that increasing the specific area of TiO2 can enhance the electron-hole separation, meanwhile, the corresponding recombination gets more, and the generated fluorescence will be stronger. The CQDs/TNWAs possessed the lowest fluorescence intensity, which reflects the existence of charge transfer between CQDs and TiO2 resulting in recombination behavior of hole-electron pairs being hindered. For further verification, the photogenerated carrier lifetime was tested by TRF as shown in Fig. 9b. The lifetime was fitted according to the Eq. (9).
(9) τ =τ1*A1% + τ2*A2% + τ3*A3%

here, τ is the lifetime, τ1, τ2, τ3 and A1, A2, A3 are the fitted life and corresponding proportion respectively. After fitting, the lifetime of photocarrier of TNS, TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs are 9.84 ns, 10.16 ns and 15.85 ns respectively, as shown in the inset of Fig. 9b. In contrast to the pure TiO2 (TNS and TNWAs), CQDs/TNWAs exhibit slower decay kinetics, implying that the CQDs could provide a transfer channel for electrons. Thus, the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs was reduced and the surface reaction efficiency was improved[77].

The UV-Vis-NIR absorption spectra of solid samples (TNS, TNWAs, CQDs/TNWAs) and liquid samples (CQDs) were tested. As shown in Fig. 9c, the CQDs has a strong absorption peak at 280 nm and its absorption boundary is near 345 nm, which is caused by the π-π * transition of the C-C bond. The absorption intensity of TNS and TNWAs are almost the same, and their absorption center all located at 303 nm and 305 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, the corresponding absorbing boundaries are estimated at 391 nm and 395 nm, respectively. With the loading of CQDs on TNWAs, the absorption center of CQDs/TNWAs slightly redshifted to 310 nm, and the absorption boundary extended to the visible region (417 nm). At this point, many researchers[42–45,63] believe that CQDs can sensitize TiO2 to achieve visible-light-driven photocatalyst. However, there is a degree of controversy on this view. Hereon, we adopted chopped photocurrent-time test to verify. The photocurrent of TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs was measured by using a xenon lamp to simulate sunlight, assembling UV cut-off filter (< 400 nm) to filter ultraviolet light. As shown in Fig. S7, under natural light, the photocurrent intensity of CQDs/TNWAs is 19% higher than that of TNWAs, however, when the UV light is filtered, the both photocurrent intensity is almost the same. Therefore, it can be concluded that the enhancement of TiO2 performance by CQDs is mainly due to the ultraviolet region, rather than the visible region, which is related to the absorption center of both TiO2 and CQDs that is both in the ultraviolet region. Therefore, the CQDs/TNWAs with visible light response are likely to be false impressions due to the visible light absorption ability of CQDs themselves, which cannot be directly transferred to TiO2. According to Eq. (10), the bandgap can be estimated.
(10) ( αhγ)n = k ( hυ-Eg)

Here, α is the absorption coefficient, h is the Planck’s constant, is photon frequency, K is a proportionality constant, Eg is bandgap and n is a constant with a value of 1/2, representing an indirect bandgap semiconductor. And the results are shown in Fig. 9d. Based on the conjugate π-π * transition of CQDs, the Eg between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) is estimated at 3.55 eV[42,45,52], confirming the absorption center is in the ultraviolet range. Moreover, CQDs showed slight absorption (380 nm ∼ 500 nm) in the visible region, which was consistent with the PL spectrum, which is attributed to a small amount of n-π * transitions. The bandgaps of the TNS, TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs are 3.25 eV, 3.24 eV and 3.12 eV respectively, indicating that the change of nano-morphology has no effect on the bandgap, and the loading of CQDs makes the bandgap decrease, but doesn’t reach to the visible region. It can be concluded that the CQDs did not change the bandgap of TiO2. In contrast, XPS results confirmed the electron transfer behavior between CQDs and TiO2, under ultraviolet light, CQDs can make a single photon provide multiple excitons, producing coupling with the photogenerated carriers of TiO2 [73]. In the case of both stimulated transitions, they become each other's charge supply body and receptors. Thus, the false impression that the bandgap changed may caused by charge transfer and difference of energy level position.

To further locate the positions of the conduction band and valence band, so as to predict the possible charge transfer mechanism, the Motschottky (M-S) curve was tested and the data was processed according to Eq. (11) [75] as shown in Fig. 9e.
(11)

here, VFB is flat-band potential, C is Interfacial capacitance, e is electronic charge, ε is the dielectric constant of the semiconductor, and ε0 is the permittivity of free space, ND is the number of donors, V is applied voltage; KB is Boltzmann’s constant and T is the absolute temperature. According to the positive slope of TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs in M-S plots, both of them are n-type semiconductors. The flat band potential is converted to 0.17 eV and 0.06 eV respectively by Eq. (2). For N-type semiconductors, the position of the conduction band (CB) is higher than the flat band potential of 0.1 ∼ 0.3 eV[15], here, 0.2 is taken. The CB of TNWAs and CQDs/TNWAs is estimated at − 0.03 eV and − 0.14 eV respectively. Using cyclic voltammetry method (CV)[76,77] to determine the LOMO state position of CQDs, Fig. 9f shows the first circle of scanned curve, and the LUMO position of CQDs was estimated to be − 0.68 V vs. NHE.

Based on the classic “Z” type reaction model, we propose a possible charge separation and transfer mechanism ( Fig. 10). According to the results of the UV-Vis-NIR absorption spectrum, both TiO2 and CQDs can absorb UV light, resulting in two kinds of excitation, one is the electronically excited transition of TiO2 that generates a hole-electron pair, the other is the π-π * transition of the conjugate structure of CQDs. It indicates that the photoelectron yield of CQDs/TNWAs is higher than that of single component TiO2. From the results of the PL discussion, part of the excited electrons of CQDs created Stocks-shift to a lower energy level through non-radiative transition, and then return to SP2 (π) and SP3 (σ) orbit by means of radiative transition. In addition, according to the PEC and XPS results, there are an electron-transfer behavior between TiO2 and CQDs, therefore, it can be inferred that another part of the excitons of CQDs are injected into the lower energy level (CB) of TiO2. From the PL and TRF results of CQDs/TNWAs, not all photoelectrons in TiO2 directly recombine with holes and transition to VB, but some electrons transfer to the HOMO level of CQDs, and that will be remotivated in CQDs. During the cycle process, some holes in VB of TiO2 are well isolated, which plays a crucial role in water splitting. Thereby, it is verified that the PEC performance of CQDs/TNWAs is significantly higher than that of single-component TiO2 species due to the isolated holes generation and more photo-generated electrons.

In conclusion, in-situ CQDs/TNWAs have been successfully prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method, and the role of CQDs in the morphological evolution of TNS to TNWAs was discussed. The results show that the CQDs played a similar role to surfactants. Through surface adsorption, the one-dimensional growth of TNW was promoted and the two-dimensional growth (including lateral growth) of TNW was inhibited, resulting in the disappearance of the compact layer. The synthesized TiO2 nanowires have a finer diameter, higher aspect ratio, more ordered array morphology and better light tolerance. Furthermore, the CQDs are naturally loaded on TNWAs, forming a CQDs/TNWAs heterojunction, which exhibited significantly better performance than the original TNS in the enhancing the separation of hole-electron pairs, the inhibiting the recombination of hole-electron pairs, the accelerating the charge transfer kinetics, and the improving the photoelectron lifetime. We further discussed the contribution of the loaded CQDs and morphology optimization. It is proved that the morphology optimization has a greater contribution to the electron-hole separation than the CQDs/TNWAs heterojunction. However, the contribution of the morphology optimization on the charge transfer efficiency is weaker than that of the CQDs/TNWAs heterojunction. Especially in photoelectron lifetime and inhibition of electron-hole recombination, morphology optimization can hardly contribute. This work presents a simple and effective strategy to optimize the morphology of TNWAs and form CQDs/TNWAs composites simultaneously, which may create a new idea for the functional application of CQDs.
